content.text: The Azure Retail Prices API is a public HTTP endpoint that exposes current Azure list prices without requiring authentication or an Azure account. It returns the same pricing data shown on the Azure Pricing Calculator and the Azure Pricing pages, but in a machine-readable JSON format suitable for automation.

You can use it to build cost estimation scripts, integrate pricing checks into CI/CD pipelines, populate internal dashboards, compare regions, or back-calculate break-even points between PAYG and reserved instances — all without any Azure SDK or credentials.

attrs.alt: Azure Retail Prices API request and response structure

content.text: All requests go to a single endpoint. The api-version query parameter is required and selects the response schema.

# Base endpoint
https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices

# Required parameter
?api-version=2023-01-01-preview

# Full example: fetch all Virtual Machine prices in East US
curl -s "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armRegionName eq 'eastus'" | python3 -m json.tool | head -80
The api-version 2023-01-01-preview is the latest stable version as of mid-2024. Microsoft may add versions; older versions remain accessible but may not include newer SKU fields.
The API supports a subset of OData $filter expressions. The operators are eq (equals), ne (not equals), and (logical AND), or (logical OR), and the function contains() for substring matching.
Only VM prices (excludes Storage, SQL, etc.)

# Combine multiple filters with 'and'
curl -sG "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices" \
  --data-urlencode "api-version=2023-01-01-preview" \
  --data-urlencode "\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, 'Linux') and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; [print(f"{i['skuName']}: \${i['retailPrice']}/hr") for i in json.load(sys.stdin)['Items']]"
The requests library is all you need — no Azure SDK, no authentication.

import requests
from typing import Optional

PRICES_URL = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
API_VERSION = "2023-01-01-preview"

def query_prices(filter_expr: str, top: int = 100) -> list:
    """Fetch all matching items, following pagination automatically."""
    params = {
        "api-version": API_VERSION,
        "$filter": filter_expr,
        "$top": top,
    }
    all_items = []
    url = PRICES_URL
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=15)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        all_items.extend(data.get("Items", []))
        url = data.get("NextPageLink")
        params = {}  # params already encoded in NextPageLink
    return all_items

# Example 1: PAYG Linux prices for all D-series VMs in eastus
d_series = query_prices(
    "serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' "
    "and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
    "and contains(productName, 'Linux') "
    "and contains(armSkuName, 'Standard_D') "
    "and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
)
for item in sorted(d_series, key=lambda x: x["retailPrice"])[:10]:
    print(f"  {item['armSkuName']:<25} ${item['retailPrice']:.4f}/hr")

# Example 2: All reservation prices for Standard_D4s_v5 worldwide
d4s_reservations = query_prices(
    "armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5' "
    "and priceType eq 'Reservation' "
    "and contains(productName, 'Linux')"
)

# Group by term and region
from collections import defaultdict
by_term = defaultdict(dict)
for item in d4s_reservations:
    term = "1yr" if "1 Year" in item.get("skuName","") else "3yr"
    region = item.get("armRegionName","")
    by_term[term][region] = item["retailPrice"]

for term, regions in by_term.items():
    print(f"\n{term} reservation ($/hr, Linux, Standard_D4s_v5):")
    for region, price in sorted(regions.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])[:8]:
        print(f"  {region:<20} ${price:.4f}/hr  (${price*8760*int(term[0]):.0f} total)")

Querying the Retail Prices API on every request adds latency and puts unnecessary load on the API. For most use cases, caching prices for 24 hours is appropriate — Azure list prices change infrequently.

import json, time, hashlib, os, requests

CACHE_DIR = "/tmp/azure-prices-cache"
CACHE_TTL_SECONDS = 86400  # 24 hours
os.makedirs(CACHE_DIR, exist_ok=True)

def cached_query(filter_expr: str) -> list:
    """Return cached results if fresh; otherwise fetch and cache."""
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(filter_expr.encode()).hexdigest()
    cache_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{cache_key}.json")

    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        age = time.time() - os.path.getmtime(cache_path)
        if age < CACHE_TTL_SECONDS:
            with open(cache_path) as f:
                data = json.load(f)
            print(f"Cache hit ({int(age/3600)}h old): {len(data)} items")
            return data

    # Cache miss — fetch from API
    print("Cache miss — fetching from API...")
    items = []
    url = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
    params = {"api-version": "2023-01-01-preview", "$filter": filter_expr}
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=15)
        data = resp.json()
        items.extend(data.get("Items", []))
        url = data.get("NextPageLink")
        params = {}

    with open(cache_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(items, f)
    print(f"Cached {len(items)} items to {cache_path}")
    return items

# Use cached prices
prices = cached_query(
    "serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and priceType eq 'Consumption' and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
)
print(f"Total VM PAYG prices in eastus: {len(prices)}")
For teams with multiple scripts that need pricing data, a small shared library avoids duplicating the fetch-and-cache logic.

# azure_prices.py — reusable price lookup module

import requests, json, time, hashlib, os

_CACHE = {}  # in-memory cache for the current process lifetime

def _fetch(filter_expr: str) -> list:
    if filter_expr in _CACHE:
        ts, items = _CACHE[filter_expr]
        if time.time() - ts < 3600:  # 1-hour in-memory TTL
            return items

    url = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
    params = {"api-version": "2023-01-01-preview", "$filter": filter_expr}
    items = []
    while url:
        r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=15)
        r.raise_for_status()
        d = r.json()
        items.extend(d.get("Items", []))
        url = d.get("NextPageLink")
        params = {}

    _CACHE[filter_expr] = (time.time(), items)
    return items

def vm_payg_price(sku: str, region: str, os_type: str = "Linux") -> float:
    """Return PAYG $/hr for a VM SKU. os_type: Linux or Windows."""
    items = _fetch(
        f"armSkuName eq 'Standard_{sku}' and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
        f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, '{os_type}') "
        f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
    )
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0.0

def vm_reservation_price(sku: str, region: str, term: str = "1 Year",
                          os_type: str = "Linux") -> float:
    """Return RI $/hr for a VM SKU."""
    items = _fetch(
        f"armSkuName eq 'Standard_{sku}' and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
        f"and priceType eq 'Reservation' and contains(productName, '{os_type}') "
        f"and contains(skuName, '{term}')"
    )
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0.0

def storage_price(sku_name: str, region: str) -> float:
    """Return $/month for a managed disk SKU (e.g. 'P10', 'E20')."""
    items = _fetch(
        f"serviceName eq 'Storage' and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
        f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(skuName, '{sku_name}')"
    )
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0.0

# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    payg = vm_payg_price("D4s_v5", "eastus")
    ri_1yr = vm_reservation_price("D4s_v5", "eastus", "1 Year")
    ri_3yr = vm_reservation_price("D4s_v5", "eastus", "3 Years")
    disk = storage_price("P10", "eastus")

    print(f"D4s_v5 Linux eastus:")
    print(f"  PAYG:      ${payg:.4f}/hr  (${payg*730:.2f}/mo)")
    print(f"  1-yr RI:   ${ri_1yr:.4f}/hr  (${ri_1yr*730:.2f}/mo, -{(1-ri_1yr/payg)*100:.0f}% vs PAYG)")
    print(f"  3-yr RI:   ${ri_3yr:.4f}/hr  (${ri_3yr*730:.2f}/mo, -{(1-ri_3yr/payg)*100:.0f}% vs PAYG)")
    print(f"  P10 disk:  ${disk:.4f}/mo")
Using skuName instead of armSkuName for exact match
Returns 0 or wrong results for some SKUs
Use armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5' for precise match
Reservation comparisons polluted by low spot prices
Forgetting productName OS filter for VMs
Returns both Windows and Linux prices mixed
Only first 100 items returned for broad queries
Returns global prices for all 60+ regions (slow)

The Retail Prices API covers all Azure services, not just VMs. Fetching prices for Storage, SQL Database, App Service, and other services follows the same pattern — only the serviceName and relevant filter fields change.

import requests

BASE = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
API_V = "2023-01-01-preview"

def fetch_first(filter_expr: str) -> dict:
    resp = requests.get(BASE, params={"api-version": API_V, "$filter": filter_expr}, timeout=10)
    items = resp.json().get("Items", [])
    return items[0] if items else {}

# Azure Blob Storage: LRS hot tier in eastus (per GB/month)
blob = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Storage' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and skuName eq 'Hot LRS' and meterName eq 'Data Stored' and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Blob Storage LRS Hot (eastus): ${blob.get('retailPrice',0):.6f}/{blob.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure SQL Database: General Purpose, 4 vCores, eastus
sql = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'SQL Database' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(skuName, 'General Purpose') and contains(skuName, '4 vCores') "
    "and priceType eq 'Consumption' and not contains(skuName, 'Serverless')"
)
print(f"SQL DB GP 4 vCores (eastus): ${sql.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{sql.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure App Service: Standard S2 plan, Windows, eastus
app = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Azure App Service' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and skuName eq 'S2' and contains(productName, 'Windows') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"App Service S2 Windows (eastus): ${app.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{app.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Kubernetes Service: node VM costs are regular VM prices
# AKS itself has no management fee — only the underlying VMs and other resources are billed
print("AKS: no management fee; bill VM SKU prices for node pools")

# Azure Load Balancer: Standard tier, data processed
lb = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Load Balancer' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(meterName, 'Data Processed') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Load Balancer data processed: ${lb.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{lb.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Key Vault: secret operations
kv = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Key Vault' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(meterName, 'Operations') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Key Vault operations: ${kv.get('retailPrice',0):.6f}/{kv.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Functions: execution time
fn = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Azure Functions' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and meterName eq 'Execution Time' and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Functions execution time: ${fn.get('retailPrice',0):.10f}/{fn.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Cosmos DB: RU/s provisioned throughput
cosmos = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Azure Cosmos DB' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(meterName, '100 RU/s') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Cosmos DB 100 RU/s: ${cosmos.get('retailPrice',0):.6f}/{cosmos.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

Azure occasionally updates list prices. A simple script that runs daily, fetches key prices, and compares against a stored baseline will alert you when pricing changes — important for maintaining accurate cost estimates in your tooling.

The Retail Prices API pairs well with Terraform plan output: parse the planned resources, look up their prices, and produce a cost estimate before applying the plan. This is the approach used by open-source tools like infracost.

import json, subprocess, requests

def get_vm_price(sku: str, region: str) -> float:
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"armSkuName eq '{sku}' and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, 'Linux') "
            f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
        ),
    }
    items = requests.get(
        "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10
    ).json().get("Items", [])
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0

def estimate_terraform_plan(plan_json_path: str) -> float:
    """Parse a Terraform plan JSON and estimate monthly compute cost."""
    with open(plan_json_path) as f:
        plan = json.load(f)

    total_monthly = 0.0
    for change in plan.get("resource_changes", []):
        rtype = change.get("type", "")
        after = change.get("change", {}).get("after") or {}

        if rtype == "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine":
            sku = after.get("size", "")
            region = after.get("location", "eastus").lower().replace(" ", "")
            price = get_vm_price(sku, region)
            monthly = price * 730
            total_monthly += monthly
            print(f"  VM: {after.get('name','?')} ({sku}, {region}) = ${monthly:.2f}/mo")

        elif rtype == "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine_scale_set":
            sku = after.get("sku", [{}])[0].get("name", "") if after.get("sku") else ""
            region = after.get("location", "eastus").lower().replace(" ", "")
            count = after.get("sku", [{}])[0].get("capacity", 1) if after.get("sku") else 1
            price = get_vm_price(sku, region)
            monthly = price * 730 * count
            total_monthly += monthly
            print(f"  VMSS: {after.get('name','?')} ({sku} x{count}) = ${monthly:.2f}/mo")

    print(f"Estimated monthly compute cost: ${total_monthly:.2f}")
    return total_monthly

# Run: terraform show -json tfplan.binary > plan.json
# Then: estimate_terraform_plan("plan.json")

This approach only covers compute; a complete pre-plan cost estimate also needs to account for storage, networking, and PaaS services. Open-source tools like infracost integrate the Retail Prices API across all Terraform resource types and output a per-resource cost breakdown in the CI pipeline.

# Azure App Service: Standard S2 plan, Windows, eastus

    "serviceName eq 'Azure App Service' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "

)
print(f"App Service S2 Windows (eastus): ${app.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{app.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Kubernetes Service: node VM costs are regular VM prices

print("AKS: no management fee; bill VM SKU prices for node pools")

# Azure Load Balancer: Standard tier, data processed
lb = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Load Balancer' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "

print(f"Load Balancer data processed: ${lb.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{lb.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Key Vault: secret operations

    "serviceName eq 'Key Vault' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(meterName, 'Operations') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Key Vault operations: ${kv.get('retailPrice',0):.6f}/{kv.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Functions: execution time
fn = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Azure Functions' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and meterName eq 'Execution Time' and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Functions execution time: ${fn.get('retailPrice',0):.10f}/{fn.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

# Azure Cosmos DB: RU/s provisioned throughput
cosmos = fetch_first(
    "serviceName eq 'Azure Cosmos DB' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
    "and contains(meterName, '100 RU/s') and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
)
print(f"Cosmos DB 100 RU/s: ${cosmos.get('retailPrice',0):.6f}/{cosmos.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

import json, subprocess, requests

def get_vm_price(sku: str, region: str) -> float:
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"armSkuName eq '{sku}' and armRegionName eq '{region}' "

            f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
        ),
    }
    items = requests.get(
        "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10
    ).json().get("Items", [])
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0

        plan = json.load(f)

        after = change.get("change", {}).get("after") or {}

            sku = after.get("size", "")
            region = after.get("location", "eastus").lower().replace(" ", "")

            region = after.get("location", "eastus").lower().replace(" ", "")

# Then: estimate_terraform_plan("plan.json")

This approach only covers compute; a complete pre-plan cost estimate also needs to account for storage, networking, and PaaS services. Open-source tools like infracost integrate the Retail Prices API across all Terraform resource types and output a per-resource cost breakdown in the CI pipeline.

When starting to query a new Azure service, you may not know the exact serviceName to filter on. A broad query without a serviceName filter returns all services; grouping by serviceName reveals the full catalogue.

import requests
from collections import Counter

# Fetch a broad sample (no serviceName filter) and list available service names
params = {
    "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
    "$filter": "armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption'",
    "$top": 100,
}
resp = requests.get("https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=15)
items = resp.json().get("Items", [])

service_counts = Counter(item.get("serviceName","") for item in items)
print("Sample of available service names (eastus, first 100 items):")
for svc, count in service_counts.most_common(20):
    print(f"  {svc}")

# To get all service names (takes multiple pages):
# Use $select to reduce payload size (not yet supported in this API version)
# Instead, use $top=100 and iterate until you have seen all services

# Quick shell one-liner: list unique service names from eastus PAYG prices
curl -s "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&\$filter=armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption'&\$top=100" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
names = sorted(set(i.get('serviceName','') for i in data['Items']))
for n in names: print(n)
"

The serviceName values are case-sensitive and must match exactly. Common pitfalls: 'Azure App Service' (not 'App Service'), 'Azure Functions' (not 'Functions'), 'SQL Database' (not 'Azure SQL Database'). When in doubt, query with $top=1 and print the full item to see the exact field values.

The Azure Retail Prices API at prices.azure.com requires no authentication and supports OData $filter queries to retrieve current PAYG, reservation, and Dev/Test prices for any Azure service. Use armSkuName for exact VM SKU matching, filter by priceType to separate PAYG from reservations, always filter by armRegionName to avoid large result sets, and paginate with NextPageLink. Cache responses for 1-24 hours and wrap the logic in a shared library to keep cost estimation scripts clean and consistent across your tooling.
